item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dunno why, but this object made me laugh, similar to the Carthage-Hating Bridge and Butt Ghost. Short and sweet, I guess, plus the note (having been on the receiving end of one too many angry ragequits in vidya).

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
It's nothing fancy, but I made a reading of this SCP. That being despite that fact that I'm not sure I 100% understand it… Like, it's kind of like an unsolvable puzzle, yes?

https://youtu.be/Pmt7HJZP5ts

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Er…I think maybe I find a spell mistake

"incorrect" result appearing to cause sigificant frustration

I think it should be significant but not sigificant , but I don't konw if it's just another word I haven't seen before . So if I'm wrong ,please forgive my rudeness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea what's going on here, or what the object is, or what it does. Neither do I know what's up with the D-class visits, or the picture, or the note, or the telepathy aspect. It's really confusing, and not in a good way.

I get that SCP-2642 is hard to describe, but I don't believe the Foundation, in-universe, would feel satisfied with the information contained in this file - even if it's hard, they'd at least try to devise a way of getting the information across to the reader.

Exceptions to this occur when the item is not viewed or attempted within five days or less.

This is a very confusing sentence. For one, I can't work out what it means. I'm also pretty sure it's grammatically incorrect - you can't "attempt" an item, and as far as I know "within five days" and "five days or less" can't be used together like that.

The way the item can only be described as half an elephant's face also feels very reminiscent of the earlier SCP-2602, which used to be a library.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What the fuck. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
People keep saying it would be better if the pic were 'half of an elephant's face'

I think it would be better if it were a FULL head. And people insisted they just see half of it… and can't even agree on what half is it or how is it cut 'cause they themseves aren't sure.

…but that's just me, I like the article as it is too, upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm interpreting this as a memetic hallucination, and it makes you feel awful because you both know and don't know that it's just a vase.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only way I would upvote this is if the actual 'secret' of the skip was that the report makes the anomaly: the official Foundation report says that it's half of an elephant's face, therefore people who view the object feel compelled to see half of an elephant's face and go crazy because it doesn't work, since it is not half of an elephant's face.
But that cannot be the case since the trouble started before containment and report.

So I simply don't understand what's going on. Shapeshifting? Pareidolia? Optical illusion? Hypnosis? Just WTF for the sake of WTF? Where was the object found (apart from in Washington State…)? I don't think the image displayed helps.
Why is it Euclid? Why must a D-class personnel view it regularly? Why isn't it locked away somewhere where no one can see it and obsess about it?
I just don't get it, because too much of the article is devoted to the compulsion, and not enough to what it is/does.

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's far too many extraneous elements going on for this to feel like a coherent narrative. There's the specific goofiness of the elephant's face thing, the completely unrelated to anything picture, I guess you hear voices if you think about it or something, I don't know. This isn't really presented very clearly to me, and in as short a format as this that's not really going to hold up.

More than that, though, I don't care for how this affects people. This is what I think of as a pseudocompulsion effect, where the article doesn't come out and say "this is a thing what compels you" because that usually brings out the brickbats, but it still behaves like one. In this article, everyone seems to have the same exact reaction, same frustration, and does the same things in response to the anomaly. That's patently uninteresting. Instead of writing something that would naturally, by its own existence, affect people in a certain way (and therefore the reader), this is a "people as robots" style exercise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
if it were, in fact, a picture of half an elephant's face, it would be clear where the bisection line falls.

think of it this way. you know it's half of an elephant's face. you just can't figure out which half

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: meh. Put me in the Djoric/thedeadlymoose camp here, it would be better if the picture were indeed half an elephant's face. It's way too straightforward. I felt like I had read this before, even though I wasn't aware of this article till a couple minutes ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm no-voting.

Mostly this is because it does nothing for me, but it does nothing for me because finding the "bisecting line" of "half an elephant's face" is not intuitively obvious to me, and even when it's been explained I can't see people sufficiently motivated to pursue attempting to find it. I understand that they are compelled to do so, but it… it just seems like a stupid thing to try to do in the first place.

So people are compelled to solve a puzzle that isn't actually even remotely a puzzle. With this particular "puzzle," that just isn't working for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's way too formulaic for me. I expected there to be an "Are We Cool Yet?" note at the bottom. Though the writing is well above average, it feels too much like 1,000 other pieces of anart I've seen written up.

It actually would be better if it had a picture of half of an elephant's face, for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Simple, sweet, effectively described and I connect totally to the "No, that's not quite right" implications. I've had documents that drove me up that wall to lay out.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would have been far better if it was actually half of an elephant's face.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My read: People view it as half an elephant's face. When there's half of something, there's a bisection point which separates one half from the other. The people viewing it become obsessed with how the halves are separated, because there are many ways to bisect things.

That's what I think it's saying. However, I am ambivalent towards the whole "things that are hard to describe" thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda left that vague on purpose. If it is, the only thing it definitely never turns into is half of an elephant's face : P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
regardless of whether it retains that form or not.

Noting the addition of the picture, I wonder if this implies that the object is shape-shifting or not?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The picture really depends. I can't really say for sure if everyone will accept that suggestion, although the irony value will be high. It's just that I feel like it's an anomaly that imposes a specific identity onto a thing.

But your call…

P.S. If that is really what this SCP is, I must say that it would be similar to the semantics dissonance chamber minus the really out-there weirdness. Yet, the two concepts can stand together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … If you don't mind, I'm gonna go ahead and use those suggestions. I didn't even really think of adding in a pic to make it more obvious. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting primarily because no, it is not cool yet. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

they are wrong.

Perhaps you would like to clarify what "wrong" means. It's quite broadly defined and is prone to many interpretations. Are you saying "incorrect"?

98 percent of cases

Question: Why not use "%" to reduce the word count?

Pros:

  • The note really caught my eye.
  • I am intrigued by the weirdness of the anomaly.

Cons:

  • If only we got a picture of it that is actually not "half an elephant's face", that would've heightened the irony of the situation and bring it to a whole level.

Verdict: Upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No-vote for the same reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really get what's going on. If it's not half an elephant's face, then why are people trying to bisect it? Hell, if it was, why would you? I'm getting really irritated by these "things we can't actually describe" entries. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed : P

Alright, thanks! If I get notifications of people thinking similarly, I'll see what I can do about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted because I reviewed the article and you didn't mention me.

In reality, I'm downvoting for the same reasons I mentioned on chat. It just feels like a series of non sequiturs to me, without a purpose for doing so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After much deliberation, I have decided to upvote. I like the base concept, but I was unsure about whether or not the object was sufficiently interesting, as other "so inexplicable it drives you crazy trying to figure it out" objects exist. For now, though, I upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorite entries in the straightforward vein of this contest. Does something simple, but does it well.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
It has been a whirlwind of a last few days, but one thing is certain: The guy who made this object hates life. Thanks to TwistedGears, Decibelles, Zolgamax, Rumetzen, ProcyonLotor and Rikks for critique, and BrandonBriggs, A_Random_Day, DolphinSlugchugger, Servbot, Roget, and weizhong for looking it over.

EDIT: Added an image, thanks to TwistedGears. Image credit: Wikimedia commons from Sailko. Someone badger me if it's worse off for it.
